item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ The hidden text, for those who can't get to Page Source
- You're welcome

RESTRICTED
MARKED HEARTS ONLY

From Perhacs-1, AKA Lucibelle, I received a lovely amount of data on extraneous epidermis growth and adaptability, namely to how it managed to cover the needles embedded inside of her.

This will be used to create the majority of Alizarin's body. To shape it. From there, I'll need to look more into Alizarin's movement, possibly using liquid from the Valley of the Mouth.

item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: The first image used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC-BY-SA 3.0) due to the presence of NC and ND in the image's license. As such, the first image has been removed.

item.title: Inconsistent pronouns
item.content:encoded:
The idea is certainly interesting, and quickly devolves into horror for the poor old lady.

As others have mentioned, though, there's some inconsistent pronoun usage (SCP-2695 is referred to as "it", "she", and "they"). I tried to correct those (as well as some grammatical issues), and elected to use the "she/her" model more consistently throughout the article.

Of course, feel free to revert my change if you'd prefer "it" or "they" instead.

item.title: Even in Montreal
item.content:encoded: I don’t think a Canadian would refer to the US as ‘America.’

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How could you do this to this poor old lady +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a great SCP that is vastly underrated.

My only complaint are the elements connecting it with a storyline outside the skip itself, which make things slightly confusing for those not familiar with the other pieces — but I think those elements aren't distracting enough to significantly impact the quality of the skip itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Made a teensy spelling correction.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't find the hidden text :(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is broken quotes (which BTW is probably the worst way to do hidden text; there remains the possibility that Wikidot will fix that bug and if they do all broken quotes will suddenly be displayed…). You can view it by clicking on +Options->Page Source and, yes, this works on mobile (I just did it with Chrome on my Android).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Where is there hidden text? I looked for it a fair bit and haven't found it, not sure if thats because I'm on mobile or not though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The dates are the same throughout both charts. Think yearly inspection. and the different designations is because the document's being revised to modern standards.

However, this concern:

Meaning, the older logs don't really sound like they're from the 20's? Definitely would like to see this cleaned up.

Will definitely address this issue when I'm no longer AFK, many thanks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am not saying it is unreasonable. I prefer it in writing (well for generic pronouns; for singular pronouns I'll use whichever the person prefers). I was only asking if the apparent anachronism was a clue, or otherwise significant (and "the Foundation uses future writing" probably counts as significant).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The current version of API says to just rephrase sentences to avoid using pronouns or use plural subjects (I know because I write sometimes for a publisher that uses API and prefers the generic "he"; which I won't use, so I end up with some tortured prose sometimes). I doubt the 1920s style guides were more progressive than the 2016 ones.

But honestly, I don't know, it seems anachronistic, but I would be pleased to learn otherwise. That is why I am asking…

EDIT:
Since you asked, from the 1918 Edition of Strunk's Elements of Style:

+ show block
– hide block

They. A common inaccuracy is the use of the plural pronoun when the antecedent is a distributive expression such as each, each one, everybody, every one, many a man, which, though implying more than one person, requires the pronoun to be in the singular. Similar to this, but with even less justification, is the use of the plural pronoun with the antecedent anybody, any one, somebody, some one, the intention being either to avoid the awkward “he or she,” or to avoid committing oneself to either. Some bashful speakers even say, “A friend of mine told me that they, etc.”

Use he with all the above words, unless the antecedent is or must be feminine.


The past isn't an especially pleasant country.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And beyond this, the Foundation has been since inception an organization wildly removed from typical societal functions and trends, due to its secretive and isolated nature. To suggest that the Foundation inches ever-so-slightly further from standard uses and definitions of words - in terms of time period or societal context or both - is not unreasonable any more than singular 'they' being a valid word is unreasonable.

Edit: Never did I say you said it's unreasonable to use singular 'they,' which you appear to be countering. I am discussing the notion that the Foundation wouldn't conform to minor outside world semantics and using a lateral example. Deepest apologies if it came across as accusatory.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Do you have any references proving they didn't?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Do you have any references for usage in formal bureaucratic writing of the period?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really excellent body horror, but the article still needs some work. Pronouns and designations are used inconsistently, there are two reports in the first chart from the same day and I'm not sure that's intentional, and I'm also not quite convinced by the time period. Meaning, the older logs don't really sound like they're from the 20's? Definitely would like to see this cleaned up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh my god she LOOKS LIKE MY GRANDMA TAKE MY UPVOTE, LEAVE MY MIMU ALONE +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is horror done right. Excellent buildup to a deeply disturbing climax that establishes a legitimately intimidating character. I'm looking forward to more of this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "I can give any amount of time, doctor." This made me so sad. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCP Foundation: torturing nice old ladies since 1919

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is for the Foundation's benefit Mark said so himself

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
:(

She seems so nice why must you do this to her

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2695: [Silence for 5 seconds.] I. Haah. T-t-tis.

Oh.

Oh no.

This is… augh, just take my upvote, goddammit. It is visceral and evocative in all the worst ways for a good night's sleep.

EDIT: ACCELERANDO I SAW THAT HIDDEN TEXT >:|

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is so fucked up and I love it. The body horror is great and I'm looking forward to see what else is in store for this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The serif font bits are very visually grating, and I feel that this is the kind of article that would have benefited from printed-and-scanned/digitally edited documents when it comes to referencing things from earlier times in the article's past. That said, it's interesting enough in terms of content for the serif font thing to be minor, and it reminds me of this mockumentary in a good way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I meant the use of "they" with a singular antecedent, which isn't officially formal writing today (yet) and I believe would have been unheard of in the 20s.

I recommend you read this article from last year, for both reasons.

Edit: Or here when it comes to the time period issue.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Whoops, my brain completely died for a minute there. Edited accordingly, thanks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I meant the use of "they" with a singular antecedent, which isn't officially formal writing today (yet) and I believe would have been unheard of in the 20s.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I used "T" because I was imagining a different method of classification around the time first article was written, whereas articles commonly use ####-A or ####-1. It's not essential to the story, though Pritchard did assign it to Lucibelle at the time he was employed under the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is the pronoun anachronism significant?

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Lucibelle Perhacs, Part 1.

For every evil under the sun,
There is a remedy, or there is none.
If there be one, try and find it;
If there be none, never mind it.
- Mother Goose


Accompaniment to listen to while reading: Mark Pritchard - ?

Image Source: Lady, Needles.

Thanks to MacLeod (for allowing me to use the idea in the first place!), Roget, HolyPowerOfMothHelm, shaggydredlocks for reviews.

Follow up with this article: SCP-2825
